system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.

Pattern 1 — Cmaj7 (Enclosure)

This first pattern is a bebop classic and is often used at the beginning of a phrase.

The main part of this pattern is a diatonic enclosure around the root of the major chord.

A diatonic enclosure is when you play one note above (the 9), then one note below (the 7), and
finally your target note (the 1).

There is a wide variety of enclosures, but atm we will focus on this particular one around the root.

The second part of pattern 1 is the 1235 motif (see pattern 36).

Pattern 2 — Dm7 (Enclosure)

Pattern 2 is the minor version of pattern 1.

Note that the pattern uses a natural 7 instead of the b7 that normally occurs in minor scales. The
natural 7 can be seen as either a chromatic note or the 7 of the melodic minor scale.

The second part of pattern 2 is the 1 2 b3 5 motif (see pattern 37).

Pattern 3 -— Short II V or Short Minor II V

Here is a classic bebop motif that works over a minor as well as a major II V I.
There are two typical bebop characteristics in this lick:

1. The triplet going from b9 to #9 to b9 with a hammer-on and pull-off.
2. The b6 interval starting from the 3rd of the dominant chord.

Here is the minor version of the lick.

Pattern 4 -— Short II V (Augmented)

This is a lick I transcribed from a Joe Pass solo and is built around a G7 augmented arpeggio (check
out pattern 35 and pattern 70 for more use of the augmented sound).

An augmented 7th arpeggio or triad is a convenient way to bring out an altered sound on dominant
chords.

Pattern 5 — G7 (Bm7b5 Sub)

The following pattern and lick use a common substitution for dominant chords: a half-diminished
arpeggio starting from the 3rd of the dominant chord.

Playing a half-diminished arpeggio from the 3rd of a dominant chord brings out the 9 sound.
For example: Bm7b5 over G7.

(c#) to Dm7 (c) to Dm6 (b).

Pattern 14 - Short Minor II V (harmonic minor)

Pattern 14 is a descending A harmonic minor run over a II VI in A minor.
On a minor ii V I, you can play the tonic harmonic minor scale over the V7alt chord.
For example: over Bm7b5-E7alt-Am7 (ii VI in A minor), you play A harmonic minor over the

E7alt chord.

When doing so, you create a 7b9,b13 sound over that chord.

Another name for the harmonic minor scale played over a dominant chord is the Phrygian dominant
scale.

| A harmonic minor = E Phrygian dominant

Pattern 18 —- Dm7 (Doubling Notes)

Pattern 18 uses a simple, but effective technique that I learned from transcribing Pat Metheny solos:

doubling notes.

It can be applied to every scale, arpeggio, or pattern you can think of.

This example (study 2 bar 22) applies the doubling technique to an 1235 pattern in D minor.

Pattern 23 —- Bm7b5 (Fmaj7 Sub)

Pattern 23 features an Fmaj7 arpeggio over Bm7b5.

The major arpeggio brings out the 11 sound, a beautiful tension over half-diminished chords.

Fmaj7 arpeggio | [|

Played over Bm7b5

You can play a maj7 chord over a half-diminished chord, starting on the b5 of that half-
diminished chord. For example: Fmaj7 over Bm7b5

Notice that the phrase follows the same major chord shape as pattern 22.

Pattern 26 — Dominant, Dominant (Minor Key), Minor
